License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.18023v2 [math-ph] 06 May 2026

Spherical singularities in compactified
Ruijsenaars–Schneider systems

Abstract.

We investigate certain Liouville integrable systems constructed earlier via reduction of the quasi-Hamiltonian double of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n). These systems live on compact connected symplectic manifolds of dimension 2​(n−1)2(n-1) and can be interpreted as compactified trigonometric Ruijsenaars–Schneider systems. Depending on the value of a parameter 0<y<π0<y<\pi, they arise in two drastically different forms: in type (i) these are toric systems, while in the type (ii) cases they possess globally continuous action variables that generate a Hamiltonian torus action (only) on a dense open subset of the phase space. The principal goal of the paper is to study those fibers of the action map (alias the 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} momentum map) which are contained in the complement of the domain of the densely defined torus action occurring in the type (ii) cases. We demonstrate that all such ‘singular fibers’ are smooth connected isotropic submanifolds. We also work out a model of the fibers as quotient spaces of certain subgroups of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) with respect to an action of another subgroup. The general results are exemplified by determining the vertices of the polytope filled by the action variables in the simplest type (ii) cases that appear for any n≥4n\geq 4 with π/(n−1)<y<π/(n−2)\pi/(n-1)<y<\pi/(n-2), and proving that the fibers over the ‘singular vertices’ are diffeomorphic to S3≃SU​(2)S^{3}\simeq\mathrm{SU}(2) in these cases. In this way, our findings enrich the set of examples of Liouville integrable systems with spherical singularities.

L. Fehéra,b and H.R. Dullinc

aDepartment of Theoretical Physics, University of Szeged, Hungary

bHUN-REN Wigner Research Centre for Physics, Budapest, Hungary

e-mail: lfeher@physx.u-szeged.hu

cSchool of Mathematics and Statistics, University of Sydney, Australia

e-mail: holger.dullin@sydney.edu.au

1. Introduction

Liouville integrable Hamiltonian systems are understood for regular values of the integral map [4]. The study of critical values and the corresponding singular fibers is an active area of research. When the integral map is the momentum map of an effective Hamiltonian action of the NN-torus on a compact connected 2​N2N-dimensional symplectic manifold, Delzant’s theorem [14] states that the image of the momentum map is an NN-dimensional convex polytope with certain additional restrictions. The preimage of a regular value in the interior of the polytope is an NN-dimensional Liouville torus. The singular values fill the lower-dimensional faces, and the corresponding singular fibers are simply tori of smaller dimension down to edges with fibers S1S^{1} and vertices with fibers just a point. The existence of an NN-torus action is very restrictive, and most integrable systems are not toric. The typical (technically speaking ‘non-degenerate’) singular fibers of Liouville integrable systems have been classified, see, e.g., [6, 49]. Relatively recently [7] so-called spherical singularities have been discovered, which posses singular fibers that are non-toral embedded submanifolds, often spheres. Even though they are degenerate singularities, they do seem to occur commonly in integrable systems studied in mathematical physics, for example in the celebrated Gelfand–Cetlin systems [8, 12, 13, 39] introduced by Guillemin and Sternberg [25]. Several systems exhibiting spherical singularities are related to toric degenerations [27, 37, 38]. For a more detailed review of general background, see [32].

The particular systems we are going to investigate were constructed in [21] by reduction of a dynamical system on SU​(n)×SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)\times\mathrm{SU}(n). On an open dense subset of their compact phase space these systems behave like the systems obeying Delzant’s theorem. The image of the ‘action map’ is a convex polytope, but some vertices violate Delzant’s conditions and we show that some of these non-Delzant vertices are spherical singularities. The pertinent systems have a physical interpretation as compactified trigonometric Ruijsenaars–Schneider systems [43, 45]. This interpretation is well known to experts on Calogero–Moser–Sutherland type integrable many-body systems, which have numerous applications in physics and connections to several areas of mathematics [16, 44]. It is a tied to a coordinate system on the phase space that we will not use in the present work. For the interpretation and the history of these systems, see also [19] and Appendix A.

We need to recall the basics of the construction of the systems of interest from [21]. It begins with the manifold

P:=SU​(n)×SU​(n)={(A,B)},P:=\mathrm{SU}(n)\times\mathrm{SU}(n)=\{(A,B)\}, (1.1)

and the map μ:P→SU​(n)\mu:P\to\mathrm{SU}(n) given by the group commutator

μ​(A,B):=A​B​A−1​B−1.\mu(A,B):=ABA^{-1}B^{-1}. (1.2)

This is an SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) equivariant map if we let SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) act on itself by conjugations and on PP by the diagonal conjugations: η⋅(A,B):=(η​A​η−1,η​B​η−1)\eta\cdot(A,B):=(\eta A\eta^{-1},\eta B\eta^{-1}), ∀η∈SU​(n)\forall\eta\in\mathrm{SU}(n). The center of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) is the set of scalar matrices z​𝟏nz{\mbox{$1$}}_{n} with zz any nn-th root of unity, which we identify with ℤn{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}. Notice that it is the adjoint group SU​(n)/ℤn\mathrm{SU}(n)/{\mathbb{Z}}_{n} that acts effectively. As a result of the equivariance, the inverse image μ−1​(μ0)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}) is stable with respect to the isotropy group SU​(n)μ0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}} for any μ0∈SU​(n)\mu_{0}\in\mathrm{SU}(n). Let us call μ0\mu_{0} a ‘strongly regular’ value of the map μ\mu if SU​(n)μ0/ℤn\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}}/{\mathbb{Z}}_{n} acts freely on μ−1​(μ0)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}). For strongly regular μ0\mu_{0}, μ−1​(μ0)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}) is an embedded submanifold of PP and the quotient space

P​(μ0):=μ−1​(μ0)/SU​(n)μ0P(\mu_{0}):=\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0})/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}} (1.3)

possesses a unique smooth111Throughout the paper, ‘smooth’ means C∞C^{\infty}. The terminology ‘strongly regular’ is justified since in our setting ‘strongly regular value’ implies regular value in the usual sense [3]. manifold structure for which the projection π0:μ−1​(μ0)→P​(μ0)\pi_{0}:\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0})\to P(\mu_{0}) is a smooth submersion. This quotient space is always compact, connected and it comes equipped with a symplectic form, denoted ωμ0\omega_{\mu_{0}}. Moreover, the conjugation invariant smooth functions of either of the matrices AA or BB descend to Hamiltonians in involution on the symplectic manifold P​(μ0)P(\mu_{0}). Choosing one of these matrices, generically one obtains (n−1)(n-1) independent Hamiltonians. The origin of the symplectic form will be illuminated in Section 2 below.

In order to obtain a Liouville integrable system P​(μ0)P(\mu_{0}) should be a smooth manifold and as small as possible. This can be achieved by taking μ0\mu_{0} to be of the form

μ0​(y):=diag​(e2​i​y,…,e2​i​y,e−2​(n−1)​i​y)\mu_{0}(y):=\mathrm{diag}\left(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y},\ldots,e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y},e^{-2(n-1){\mathrm{i}}y}\right) (1.4)

with a suitable real parameter 0≤y≤π0\leq y\leq\pi. We call yy admissible if μ0​(y)\mu_{0}(y) is strongly regular and is not a multiple of the unit matrix 𝟏n{\mbox{$1$}}_{n}. This is always the case except for the finite set of excluded values [21] for which

e2​m​i​y=1for somem=1,…,n.e^{2m{\mathrm{i}}y}=1\quad\hbox{for some}\quad m=1,\dots,n. (1.5)

For admissible yy, dim(P​(μ0​(y)))=2​(n−1)\dim(P(\mu_{0}(y)))=2(n-1).

We choose to consider the integrable system on P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)) obtained by using the conjugation invariant globally smooth functions of BB to define the commuting Hamiltonians. For any admissible yy, action variables for this system are encapsulated by an ‘action map’

(β^1,…,β^n−1):P​(μ0​(y))→ℝn−1(\hat{\beta}_{1},\dots,\hat{\beta}_{n-1}):P(\mu_{0}(y))\to{\mathbb{R}}^{n-1} (1.6)

constructed in [21] using essentially the eigenvalues of BB. The β^i\hat{\beta}_{i} are smooth functions either on the whole of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)) or on a dense open submanifold thereof, and are always globally continuous. They form the momentum map for an effective action of the torus 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}, at least on a dense open submanifold. Whether this dense open submanifold is the whole of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)) or a proper subset, depends on the value of yy. This dichotomy classifies the admissible cases (values of yy) as type (i) and type (ii). In the type (i) cases one has a globally defined 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action, and P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)) is a toric manifold, actually isomorphic with the standard toric manifold ℂ​Pn−1\mathbb{C}P^{n-1} (equipped with a yy-dependent multiple of the Fubini-Study symplectic form).

The following proposition is a reformulation and slight completion of a result from [21]. For this, let us recall that the Farey sequence 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n} of order nn is the set of all fractions 0≤a/b≤10\leq a/b\leq 1 with gcd⁡(a,b)=1\gcd(a,b)=1 and b≤nb\leq n written in increasing order (see, for example, [28, 42]) A pair of two consecutive elements in 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n} are referred to as Farey neighbours. We say that a subinterval of [0,π][0,\pi] is of type (i) if it entirely consists of type (i) values of yy, and similarly for type (ii). Equivalently, we refer to type (i) and type (ii) subintervals of [0,1][0,1] using y/πy/\pi as the parameter.

Proposition 1.1.

The Farey sequence 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n} gives the excluded values for y/πy/\pi. For 1≤κ≤(n−1)1\leq\kappa\leq(n-1) coprime to nn, the two open intervals between κ/n\kappa/n and its neighbours in 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n} are of type (i), and all other intervals between neighbours in 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n} are of type (ii).

Remark 1.2.

For 1≤κ≤(n−1)1\leq\kappa\leq(n-1) coprime to nn, the Farey neighbours of κ/n\kappa/n are a/ba/b and c/dc/d, where a,b,c,da,b,c,d with the conditions 1≤b,d≤(n−1)1\leq b,d\leq(n-1) are the unique integer solutions of the equations

b​κ−a​n=1andn​c−κ​d=1.b\kappa-an=1\quad\hbox{and}\quad nc-\kappa d=1. (1.7)

For small nn, the type (i) and type (ii) intervals, are illustrated by Figure 1. There and below, we restrict to 0≤y≤π/20\leq y\leq\pi/2, since the systems associated to yy and (π−y)(\pi-y) are isomorphic [21]. It can be shown that the number of type (i) and type (ii) intervals grows as O​(n)O(n) and as O​(n2)O(n^{2}), respectively, so type (ii) is rather the rule than the exception. These statements follow easily from standard number theoretic results [28]. See also Appendix B.

The image of the momentum map β^\hat{\beta} (1.6) is a convex polytope of dimension (n−1)(n-1) for every admissible parameter yy. This polytope, denoted 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, is defined by 2​n2n inequalities (2.25) in an (n−1)(n-1)-dimensional hyperplane in ℝn{\mathbb{R}}^{n} and is contained in the simplex 𝒜{\mathcal{A}} (2.9). It is known [21] that the fiber of β^\hat{\beta} over any interior point of 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} is a Liouville torus 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}, and a lower dimensional torus for those points of the boundary ∂𝒜y\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y} that lie in the interior 𝒜reg⊂𝒜{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}\subset{\mathcal{A}} (2.9) (in particular, a fixed point for any vertex belonging to 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}). In the type (i) cases the polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} is an (n−1)(n-1)-dimensional simplex in 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}, which is the simplest example of a Delzant polytope [5, 14]. The differentiability of β^\hat{\beta} is lost at β^−1​(∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}}), which gives the non-empty complement of the domain of the torus action in every type (ii) case.

The goal of the present paper is to initiate the study of the momentum polytopes 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} for the type (ii) cases and to uncover the structure of the fibers of the momentum map β^\hat{\beta} over the ‘singular points’ that form ∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}}. With full details contained in the text, our main result can be formulated as follows.

Theorem 1.3.

For every type (ii) parameter yy, each fiber of the momentum map β^\hat{\beta} is a smooth connected isotropic submanifold of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)). Specifically, for every ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is diffeomorphic to a smooth quotient space SU​(n)δ​(ξ)/SU​(n)ξu0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\xi}^{u_{0}}, where SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} is the isotropy group of δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) (2.10) with respect to conjugations, and SU​(n)ξu0<SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\xi}^{u_{0}}<\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} is a subgroup defined in Section 3 together with its action (3.35) on SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}.

It will be shown that the theorem recovers known results for ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}, i.e. that in these cases the fibers are tori of various dimensions, and is genuinely new for ξ∈∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜\xi\in\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}}. The interesting structure of the fibers over the ‘singular boundary’ ∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}} will be illustrated by examples.

Proposition 1.4.

For SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) with any n≥4n\geq 4, the smallest type (ii) parameters vary in the interval π/(n−1)<y<π/(n−2)\pi/(n-1)<y<\pi/(n-2). In these cases ∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}} contains only vertices, n​(n−3)n(n-3) of them, and the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is diffeomorphic to S3≃SU​(2)S^{3}\simeq\mathrm{SU}(2) for each of these vertices.

Content.

Section 2 is a summary of the necessary prerequisites from [21]. The heart of the paper is Section 3.1, where we derive the above claimed properties of the fibers of the momentum map. The detailed form of the main result is given by Theorems 3.15 and 3.17, together with Definition 3.7 and Lemma 3.11. The construction and the results given in this section are valid for any ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. It is explained in Section 3.2 how they reproduce the known structure of the fibers for ξ\xi belonging to the interior 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}} of the simplex 𝒜{\mathcal{A}} (2.9). Sections 4 and 5 are devoted to examples. In Section 4, we describe the momentum polytopes and the fibers corresponding to its singular vertices (i.e., vertices in ∂𝒜\partial{\mathcal{A}}) in the lowest dimension where type (ii) cases occur. Our new results concerning these examples are summarized by Propositions 4.3 and 4.5. Section 5.1 contains a generalization of results of the previous examples to the simplest type (ii) cases associated with SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) for any n≥4n\geq 4. In particular, we give the vertices of 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} in Theorem 5.1 for yy in the range (5.1), and describe the fibers over the n​(n−3)n(n-3) singular vertices in Theorem 5.4. Section 5.2 contains miscellaneous further results about 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} for other ranges of the parameter yy for various specific values of nn. We conclude in Section 6 by giving a summary and an outlook towards open problems. Four appendices complement the main text. Appendix A describes the connection to the compactified trigonometric Ruijsenaars–Schneider model. Appendix B elaborates some details regarding Proposition 1.1. Appendix C explains the irrelevant nature of various choices that appear in the construction in Section 3.1, and Appendix D contains the outcome of numerical explorations of the polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}.

2. Background material

The construction of [21] was formulated in the setting of quasi-Hamiltonian geometry [3]. We below highlight the rudiments of the general theory that are needed for our purpose, focusing on the relevant example of the ‘internally fused double’ of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n). This prepares the ground for presenting the description of the type (ii) momentum polytopes found in [21], which will serve as the starting point of our investigation in this paper.

2.1. Dynamics on the internally fused double

A quasi-Hamiltonian structure for a compact Lie group KK is given by a KK-manifold PP equipped with a KK-invariant 2-form ω\omega and an equivariant momentum map μ:P→K\mu:P\to K, subject to a delicate set of axioms [3]. The equivariance refers to the conjugation action of KK on itself. Here we do not detail the precise definition, just recall basic consequences [3] that make this concept useful from the perspective of Hamiltonian mechanics.

First, let μ0\mu_{0} be a regular value of the (quasi-Hamiltonian) momentum map μ\mu and suppose that there is only a single orbit type for the action of Kμ0K_{\mu_{0}} on μ−1​(μ0)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}). Then P​(μ0)=μ−1​(μ0)/Kμ0P(\mu_{0})=\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0})/K_{\mu_{0}} is a symplectic manifold with the symplectic form ωμ0\omega_{\mu_{0}} verifying the equality

ι0∗​ω=π0∗​ωμ0,\iota_{0}^{*}\omega=\pi_{0}^{*}\omega_{\mu_{0}}, (2.1)

where ι0\iota_{0} and π0\pi_{0} are the canonical inclusion and projection maps defined on μ−1​(μ0)⊂P\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0})\subset P. This coincides with the familiar relation of Marsden–Weinstein-Meyer symplectic reduction [40], although ω\omega is neither closed nor non-degenerate in general (but its kernel and exterior derivative are controlled by the axioms [3]). Second, one can associate a unique vector field VHV_{H} on PP to any KK-invariant function H∈C∞​(P)KH\in C^{\infty}(P)^{K} by requiring the usual relation ιVH​ω=d​H\iota_{V_{H}}\omega=dH complemented by the condition that D​μ​(VH)=0D\mu(V_{H})=0 holds everywhere. The resulting ‘quasi-Hamiltonian vector field’ VHV_{H} is KK-invariant and μ\mu is constant along its integral curves. Moreover, the projection of VHV_{H} on P​(μ0)P(\mu_{0}) is the Hamiltonian vector field of the ‘reduced Hamiltonian’ ℋ∈C∞​(P​(μ0)){\mathcal{H}}\in C^{\infty}(P(\mu_{0})) that enjoys the relation

H∘ι0=ℋ∘π0.H\circ\iota_{0}={\mathcal{H}}\circ\pi_{0}. (2.2)

Third, C∞​(P)KC^{\infty}(P)^{K} is a genuine Poisson algebra with the Poisson bracket of two invariant functions defined by {F,H}:=d​F​(VH)\{F,H\}:=dF(V_{H}). In short, Hamiltonian mechanics and reduction works for invariant functions on quasi-Hamiltonian manifolds in the same way as for all functions on symplectic manifolds.

Turning to the relevant example, let us introduce the following inner product on the Lie algebra 𝔰​𝔲​(n){\mathfrak{su}}(n),

⟨X,Y⟩:=−12​tr​(X​Y),∀X,Y∈𝔰​𝔲​(n),\langle X,Y\rangle:=-\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{tr}(XY),\quad\forall X,Y\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n), (2.3)

and define the 2-form ω\omega on PP (1.1) by the formula

ω:=⟨A−1​d​A,∧d​B​B−1⟩+⟨d​A​A−1,∧B−1​d​B⟩−⟨(A​B)−1​d​(A​B),∧(B​A)−1​d​(B​A)⟩.\omega:=\langle A^{-1}\mathrm{d}A\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\wedge}}{{,}}\mathrm{d}BB^{-1}\rangle+\langle\mathrm{d}AA^{-1}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\wedge}}{{,}}B^{-1}\mathrm{d}B\rangle-\langle(AB)^{-1}\mathrm{d}(AB)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\wedge}}{{,}}(BA)^{-1}\mathrm{d}(BA)\rangle. (2.4)

With this 2-form and the momentum map μ\mu (1.2), P=SU​(n)×SU​(n)P=\mathrm{SU}(n)\times\mathrm{SU}(n) becomes a quasi-Hamiltonian manifold, known as the internally fused double of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n). It represents an analogue of the cotangent bundle T∗​SU​(n)T^{*}\mathrm{SU}(n) in the quasi-Hamiltonian world [3].

For any real function h∈C∞​(SU​(n))h\in C^{\infty}(\mathrm{SU}(n)), define its 𝔰​𝔲​(n){\mathfrak{su}}(n)-valued derivative ∇h\nabla h by the relation

⟨∇h​(g),X⟩=dd​t|t=0​h​(et​X​g),∀X∈𝔰​𝔲​(n).\langle\nabla h(g),X\rangle=\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}h(e^{tX}g),\qquad\forall X\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n). (2.5)

Then, take an invariant function h∈C∞​(SU​(n))SU​(n)h\in C^{\infty}(\mathrm{SU}(n))^{\mathrm{SU}(n)} and consider the functions ha,hb∈C∞​(P)SU​(n)h_{a},h_{b}\in C^{\infty}(P)^{\mathrm{SU}(n)} for which

ha​(A,B):=h​(A),hb​(A,B)=h​(B).h_{a}(A,B):=h(A),\qquad h_{b}(A,B)=h(B). (2.6)

The integral curve of the quasi-Hamiltonian vector field of the function hah_{a} with initial value (A0,B0)(A_{0},B_{0}) can be written as

(A​(t),B​(t))=(A0,B0​e−t​∇h​(A0))(A(t),B(t))=(A_{0},B_{0}e^{-t\nabla h(A_{0})}) (2.7)

and for hbh_{b} the integral curve can be written as

(A​(t),B​(t))=(A0​et​∇h​(B0),B0).(A(t),B(t))=(A_{0}e^{t\nabla h(B_{0})},B_{0}). (2.8)

Letting ℌa{\mathfrak{H}}_{a} and ℌb{\mathfrak{H}}_{b} denote the sets of functions {ha}\{h_{a}\} and {hb}\{h_{b}\}, we see that these are two Abelian Poisson algebras of C∞​(P)SU​(n)C^{\infty}(P)^{\mathrm{SU}(n)}, and both have functional dimension (n−1)(n-1). Upon reduction, they give rise to the Abelian Poisson algebras ℌ^a\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{a} and ℌ^b\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{b} on any quotient space P​(μ0)=μ−1​(μ0)/SU​(n)μ0P(\mu_{0})=\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0})/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}}, which is in general a stratified symplectic space. It is clear that these two Abelian Poisson algebras are equivalent. In fact, they can be converted into each other by a certain ‘duality automorphism’ [3, 19] of the double PP (1.1).

2.2. Action variables before reduction

Below, we recall a parametrization of the conjugacy classes of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) and utilize it for defining (n−1)(n-1) invariant functions on PP (1.1) that are smooth on a dense open subset and generate mutually commuting 2​π2\pi-periodic flows.

It will be convenient for us to formulate the parametrization using the (n−1)(n-1)-dimensional simplex 𝒜⊂ℝn{\mathcal{A}}\subset{\mathbb{R}}^{n} given by

𝒜={ξ=(ξ1,…,ξn)∈ℝn∣ξ1+⋯+ξn=π,ξi≥0,∀i=1,…,n}.{\mathcal{A}}=\{\xi=(\xi_{1},\dots,\xi_{n})\in{\mathbb{R}}^{n}\mid\xi_{1}+\cdots+\xi_{n}=\pi,\,\,\xi_{i}\geq 0,\,\forall i=1,\dots,n\}. (2.9)

It is well known that any g∈SU​(n)g\in\mathrm{SU}(n) is conjugate to a unique diagonal matrix of the form

δ​(ξ):=exp⁡(−2​i​∑j=1n−1ξj​λj),ξ∈𝒜,\delta(\xi):=\exp\left(-2{\mathrm{i}}\sum_{j=1}^{n-1}\xi_{j}\lambda_{j}\right),\quad\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}, (2.10)

where the λj\lambda_{j} (j=1,…,n−1j=1,\dots,n-1) are the n×nn\times n matrices

λj:=∑k=1jEk,k−jn​𝟏n\lambda_{j}:=\sum_{k=1}^{j}E_{k,k}-\frac{j}{n}{\mbox{$1$}}_{n} (2.11)

with the elementary diagonal matrices Ek,kE_{k,k} and the unit matrix 𝟏n{\mbox{$1$}}_{n}. The matrix δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) can also be displayed as

δ​(ξ)=diag​(δ1​(ξ),…,δn​(ξ)),δ1​(ξ):=e2​in​∑j=1nj​ξjδk+1​(ξ):=e2​i​ξk​δk​(ξ),\delta(\xi)=\mathrm{diag}(\delta_{1}(\xi),\ldots,\delta_{n}(\xi)),\quad\delta_{1}(\xi):=e^{\frac{2{\mathrm{i}}}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}j\xi_{j}}\quad\delta_{k+1}(\xi):=e^{2{\mathrm{i}}\xi_{k}}\delta_{k}(\xi), (2.12)

where δn+1:=δ1\delta_{n+1}:=\delta_{1}. This is a special case of the parametrization of the conjugacy classes of any connected and simply connected, compact Lie group with simple Lie algebra by a Weyl alcove [17]. (In the general case, the matrices λj\lambda_{j} are replaced by the fundamental coweights of the corresponding Lie algebra.)

Now, writing g1∼g2g_{1}\sim g_{2} if the two matrices g1g_{1} and g2g_{2} are similar, we define the surjective SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) invariant map Ξ:SU​(n)→𝒜\Xi:\mathrm{SU}(n)\to{\mathcal{A}} by setting

Ξ​(g):=ξifg∼δ​(ξ).\Xi(g):=\xi\quad\hbox{if}\quad g\sim\delta(\xi). (2.13)

The dense subset SU​(n)reg\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}} of regular elements, matrices with nn distinct eigenvalues, is mapped onto the interior of 𝒜{\mathcal{A}}, denoted 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}. The map Ξ\Xi is globally continuous and its restriction on SU​(n)reg\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}} is C∞C^{\infty} (even real-analytic). At any point of Ξ−1​(∂𝒜)\Xi^{-1}(\partial{\mathcal{A}}), some component functions of Ξ\Xi lose their differentiability. In fact [17], the map Ξ\Xi descends to a homeomorphism from SU​(n)/AdSU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)/\mathrm{Ad}_{\mathrm{SU}(n)} onto 𝒜{\mathcal{A}} and its restriction to regular elements yields a diffeomorphism from SU​(n)reg/AdSU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}/\mathrm{Ad}_{\mathrm{SU}(n)} onto 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}.

Next, we introduce the map β:P→𝒜\beta:P\to{\mathcal{A}} by

β​(A,B):=Ξ​(B).\beta(A,B):=\Xi(B). (2.14)

The independent component functions are β1,…,βn−1\beta_{1},\dots,\beta_{n-1}; βn\beta_{n} is included for notational convenience. These functions are invariant under the conjugation action of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n), and their quasi-Hamiltonian vector fields generate mutually commuting flows on SU​(n)×SU​(n)reg\mathrm{SU}(n)\times\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}. To describe the flows, for t=(t1,…,tn−1)∈ℝn−1t=(t_{1},\dots,t_{n-1})\in{\mathbb{R}}^{n-1} we let ϱ​(t)\varrho(t) denote the following element of the standard maximal torus of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n):

ϱ​(t):=exp⁡(i​∑j=1n−1tj​(Ej+1,j+1−Ej,j)),\varrho(t):=\exp\left({\mathrm{i}}\sum_{j=1}^{n-1}t_{j}(E_{j+1,j+1}-E_{j,j})\right), (2.15)

and for every B∈SU​(n)regB\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}} choose a ‘diagonalizer’ g​(B)∈U​(n)g(B)\in\mathrm{U}(n) for which

g​(B)​B​g​(B)−1=δ​(Ξ​(B)).g(B)Bg(B)^{-1}=\delta(\Xi(B)). (2.16)

Notice that ϱ\varrho gives an isomorphism from 𝕋n−1=ℝn−1/(2​π​ℤ)n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}={\mathbb{R}}^{n-1}/(2\pi{\mathbb{Z}})^{n-1} onto the maximal torus of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n). Then, we find that the joint flow of the commuting quasi-Hamiltonian vector fields VβjV_{\beta_{j}} (j=1,…,n−1j=1,\dots,n-1) operates on the initial value (A,B)(A,B) according to the map

Ψtb:(A,B)↦(A​g​(B)−1​ϱ​(t)​g​(B),B).\Psi^{b}_{t}:(A,B)\mapsto(Ag(B)^{-1}\varrho(t)g(B),B). (2.17)

This represents a free action of 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} on SU​(n)×SU​(n)reg\mathrm{SU}(n)\times\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}.

Since βj=Ξj∘π2\beta_{j}=\Xi_{j}\circ\pi_{2}, the formula (2.17) follows from (2.8) and the equality

∇Ξj​(B)=g​(B)−1​(i​(Ej+1,j+1−Ej,j))​g​(B),∀j=1,…,n−1,∀B∈SU​(n)reg,\nabla\Xi_{j}(B)=g(B)^{-1}({\mathrm{i}}(E_{j+1,j+1}-E_{j,j}))g(B),\qquad\forall j=1,\dots,n-1,\,\,\forall B\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}, (2.18)

which is easily verified. Although g​(B)g(B) is well-defined only up to left-multiplication by an arbitrary diagonal unitary matrix, the ambiguity drops out from the (2.18). If desired, one may take g​(B)g(B) from SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n), and locally it can be chosen as a smooth function of B∈SU​(n)regB\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}.

We can view the functions β1,…,βn−1\beta_{1},\dots,\beta_{n-1} as action variables associated with the Abelian Poisson algebra ℌb=π2∗​C∞​(SU​(n))SU​(n){\mathfrak{H}}_{b}=\pi_{2}^{*}C^{\infty}(\mathrm{SU}(n))^{\mathrm{SU}(n)} consisting of globally smooth invariant Hamiltonians. Indeed, it is immediate from the definitions that any H∈ℌbH\in{\mathfrak{H}}_{b} can be expressed in terms of these functions, as H=χ∘βH=\chi\circ\beta with a uniquely defined function χ\chi on 𝒜{\mathcal{A}}, and the βj\beta_{j} generate mutually commuting 2​π2\pi-periodic flows on β−1​(𝒜reg)\beta^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}). The only non-standard feature is that PP is not a symplectic, but a quasi-Hamiltonian manifold.

Of course, one can similarly define the map α​(A,B):=Ξ​(A)\alpha(A,B):=\Xi(A), which can be used to generate a free 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action on SU​(n)reg×SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mathrm{reg}}\times\mathrm{SU}(n) and serves as a collection of action variables behind the Abelian Poisson algebra ℌa=π1∗​C∞​(SU​(n))SU​(n){\mathfrak{H}}_{a}=\pi_{1}^{*}C^{\infty}(\mathrm{SU}(n))^{\mathrm{SU}(n)}.

2.3. The type (ii) momentum polytopes

Let us suppose that 0<y<π0<y<\pi is an admissible value, so that

P​(μ0​(y))=μ−1​(μ0​(y))/SU​(n)μ0​(y)P(\mu_{0}(y))=\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} (2.19)

is a connected compact symplectic manifold of dimension 2​(n−1)2(n-1). In all these cases, the Abelian Poisson algebras ℌa{\mathfrak{H}}_{a} and ℌb{\mathfrak{H}}_{b} reduce to Abelian Poisson algebras of functional dimension (n−1)(n-1) on P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)), denoted ℌ^a\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{a} and ℌ^b\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{b}, which represent two Liouville integrable systems. These two systems are equivalent and we focus on ℌ^b\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{b}.

Let us define the map β^:P​(μ0​(y))→𝒜\hat{\beta}:P(\mu_{0}(y))\to{\mathcal{A}} by the prescription

β∘ι0=β^∘π0,\beta\circ\iota_{0}=\hat{\beta}\circ\pi_{0}, (2.20)

where the notation is the same as in (2.2). Then, the map

(β^1,…,β^n−1):P​(μ0​(y))→ℝn−1(\hat{\beta}_{1},\dots,\hat{\beta}_{n-1}):P(\mu_{0}(y))\to{\mathbb{R}}^{n-1} (2.21)

serves as an action map for the integrable system having the globally smooth commuting Hamiltonians that form ℌ^b\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{b}. This means that all elements of ℌ^b\hat{\mathfrak{H}}_{b} can be expressed in terms of the functions β^1,…,β^n−1\hat{\beta}_{1},\dots,\hat{\beta}_{n-1}, and they generate a Hamiltonian action of 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} on the dense222It is not entirely obvious that β^−1​(𝒜reg)⊂P​(μ0​(y))\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}})\subset P(\mu_{0}(y)) is always dense, but this is proved in [21]. open subset β^−1​(𝒜reg)\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}) of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)). Instead of the map (2.21), we also call the equivalent map β^\hat{\beta} (2.20) action map. Moreover, the terms ‘action map’ and ‘momentum map’ are used interchangeably throughout the paper. These issues should not cause any confusion.

Recall that an admissible yy is called type (ii) value if β^−1​(𝒜reg)⊂P​(μ0​(y))\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}})\subset P(\mu_{0}(y)) is a proper subset. The complementary type (i) cases are characterized by Theorem 1.1, and are completely understood. Considering SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) and a type (ii) parameter yy, we have to assume that n≥4n\geq 4 and

k​πn<y<(k+1)​πnfor somek∈{1,2,…,n−2}.k\frac{\pi}{n}<y<(k+1)\frac{\pi}{n}\quad\hbox{for some}\quad k\in\{1,2,\dots,n-2\}. (2.22)

The precise range of type (ii) parameters, including for which intervals (2.22) they occur, is given (somewhat implicitly) by Theorem 1.1. Next, we recall a characterization of the image

𝒜y:=β^​(P​(μ0​(y))).{\mathcal{A}}_{y}:=\hat{\beta}(P(\mu_{0}(y))). (2.23)
Theorem 2.1 ([21]).

Consider P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)) for n≥4n\geq 4 with a type (ii) admissible value of yy satisfying (2.22). Introduce the (n−1)(n-1)-dimensional hyperplane E⊂ℝnE\subset{\mathbb{R}}^{n} as

E:={ξ∈ℝn∣ξ1+⋯+ξn=π}E:=\{\xi\in{\mathbb{R}}^{n}\mid\xi_{1}+\dots+\xi_{n}=\pi\} (2.24)

and extend the definition of the coordinates ξj\xi_{j} nn-periodically, i.e., set ξj=ξj+n\xi_{j}=\xi_{j+n} for all j∈ℤj\in{\mathbb{Z}}. Then, the image 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} of the action map β^\hat{\beta} is the set of points of EE for which the following 2​n2n inequalities hold:

ξℓ+ξℓ+1+⋯+ξℓ+k−1≤yandξℓ+ξℓ+1+⋯+ξℓ+k≥y,∀ℓ=1,…,n.\xi_{\ell}+\xi_{\ell+1}+\cdots+\xi_{\ell+k-1}\leq y\quad\hbox{and}\quad\xi_{\ell}+\xi_{\ell+1}+\cdots+\xi_{\ell+k}\geq y,\quad\forall\ell=1,\dots,n. (2.25)
Remark 2.2.

The two inequalities for fixed ℓ\ell imply hat ξℓ+k≥0\xi_{\ell+k}\geq 0, i.e., the convex polyhedron in EE defined by the inequalities (2.25) is contained in 𝒜{\mathcal{A}} (2.9), as it must be the case. In particular, 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} is a polytope, since it is bounded. The interior of 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} contains the point ξ∗∈𝒜reg\xi^{*}\in{\mathcal{A}}_{\mathrm{reg}} whose components are

ξj∗=πn for allj=1,…,n.\xi^{*}_{j}=\frac{\pi}{n}\quad\hbox{ for all}\quad j=1,\dots,n. (2.26)

Indeed, this point satisfies (2.25) with strict inequalities, and thus 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} also contains a neighbourhood of ξ∗\xi^{*} in EE (2.24). By the definition of yy being type (ii), the (n−1)(n-1)-dimensional polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} intersects the boundary ∂𝒜\partial{\mathcal{A}}. Define the action of the cyclic permutation σ∈Sn\sigma\in{\mathrm{S}}_{n} on EE by

σ​(ξ)j:=ξj+1,\sigma(\xi)_{j}:=\xi_{j+1}, (2.27)

and observe that σ\sigma preserves the inequalities (2.25). As a result, σ\sigma maps 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} to 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, and it maps vertices to vertices, edges to edges and so on. This cyclic permutation symmetry of the polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} goes back to the identity

Ξ​(g​e−2​i​π/n)=σ​(Ξ​(g)),∀g∈SU​(n),\Xi(ge^{-2{\mathrm{i}}\pi/n})=\sigma(\Xi(g)),\quad\forall g\in\mathrm{SU}(n), (2.28)

enjoyed by the map Ξ\Xi (2.13), and the fact that if (A,B)∈μ−1​(μ0​(y))(A,B)\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) then so is (A,B​e−2​i​π/n)(A,Be^{-2{\mathrm{i}}\pi/n}).

For completeness, let us recall from [21] what happens in the type (i) cases, when yy varies between k​πnk\frac{\pi}{n} with gcd⁡(k,n)=1\gcd(k,n)=1 and one of its neighbours in the Farey sequence 𝔉n{\mathfrak{F}}_{n}. In fact, then the conditions (2.25) are still valid, but those nn inequalities that contain sums of kk terms imply the other nn inequalities, and 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} becomes a simplex in the interior of 𝒜{\mathcal{A}} (2.9).

3. The structure of the fibers of the momentum map

Let us decompose the polytope 𝒜y=β^​(P​(μ0​(y))){\mathcal{A}}_{y}=\hat{\beta}(P(\mu_{0}(y))) as

𝒜y=𝒜yo∪∂𝒜y.{\mathcal{A}}_{y}={\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o}\cup\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. (3.1)

The interior 𝒜yo{\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o} lies in 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}, while we further decompose the boundary ∂𝒜y\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y} as

∂𝒜y=(∂𝒜y)reg∪(∂𝒜y)singwith(∂𝒜y)sing:=∂𝒜y∩∂𝒜.\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}=(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{reg}}\cup(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}\quad\hbox{with}\quad(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}:=\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}}. (3.2)

For ξ∈𝒜yo\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o}, the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is a standard Liouville torus 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}, and for ξ∈(∂𝒜y)reg\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{reg}} it is an isotropic torus of smaller dimension. This follows directly from the results of [21], noting that the 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action (2.17) descends to β^−1​(𝒜y∩𝒜reg)\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}). Being orbits of a torus action, these fibers are embedded submanifolds.

3.1. The singular fibers are smooth isotropic submanifolds

In this section, we will prove that also the ‘singular fibers’

β^−1​(ξ)=(β−1​(ξ)∩μ−1​(μ0​(y)))/SU​(n)μ0​(y)forξ∈(∂𝒜y)sing\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi)=\left(\beta^{-1}(\xi)\cap\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\right)/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\quad\hbox{for}\quad\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}} (3.3)

are embedded isotropic submanifolds of the phase space P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)). More specifically, we shall see that every fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) can be identified as a quotient space of the isotropy group SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} by an action of a subgroup. Our construction will involve certain choices, but those will turn out irrelevant, since a subset can only have a single embedded submanifold structure.

Below, it is assumed that 0<y<π0<y<\pi is a type (ii) admissible parameter. We shall first consider the corresponding fiber β−1​(ξ)\beta^{-1}(\xi) in μ−1​(μ0​(y))\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)). The final outcome of our analysis is given by Theorems 3.15 and 3.17 together with Lemma 3.11, using the notations introduced in Definition 3.7. The reader will notice that the subsequent analysis is valid for any ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. It is a good check that for ξ∈(𝒜y∩𝒜reg)\xi\in({\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}) we shall recover the known result.

For any column vector u∈ℂnu\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n} of unit length, we introduce

μ^​(y,u):=e2​i​y​𝟏n+(e−2​(n−1)​i​y−e2​i​y)​u​u†,\hat{\mu}(y,u):=e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}{\mbox{$1$}}_{n}+(e^{-2(n-1){\mathrm{i}}y}-e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y})uu^{\dagger}, (3.4)

which is an element of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n). Incidentally, for e2​n​i​y≠1e^{2n{\mathrm{i}}y}\neq 1, μ^​(y,u)\hat{\mu}(y,u) as given by (3.4) has unit determinant precisely if u†​u=1u^{\dagger}u=1.

Lemma 3.1.

For any (A,B)∈μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)(A,B)\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) there exist elements u∈ℂnu\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n} and g∈U​(n)g\in\mathrm{U}(n) with gj​n=ujg_{jn}=u_{j} for j=1,…,nj=1,\dots,n such that δ​(ξ)∼μ^​(y,u)​δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi)\sim\hat{\mu}(y,u)\delta(\xi) and the pair (A,B)(A,B) has the form

(A,B)=(g−1​A^​g,g−1​δ​(ξ)​g),(A,B)=(g^{-1}\hat{A}g,g^{-1}\delta(\xi)g), (3.5)

where A^\hat{A} is an element of SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n) satisfying the condition

A^​δ​(ξ)​A^−1=μ^​(y,u)​δ​(ξ).\hat{A}\delta(\xi)\hat{A}^{-1}=\hat{\mu}(y,u)\delta(\xi). (3.6)
Proof.

Suppose that

A​B​A−1​B−1=μ0​(y)ABA^{-1}B^{-1}=\mu_{0}(y) (3.7)

and take a unitary matrix for which g​B​g−1=δ​(ξ)gBg^{-1}=\delta(\xi). Defining A^:=g​A​g−1\hat{A}:=gAg^{-1} and letting uu denote the last column of gg, the condition (3.7) is readily seen to be equivalent to (3.6), because one has due to (1.4):

g​μ0​(y)​g−1=μ^​(y,u).g\mu_{0}(y)g^{-1}=\hat{\mu}(y,u). (3.8)

∎

Lemma 3.2.

The intersection μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) is the set of elements of the form

(A,B)=(g−1​A^u​T​g,g−1​δ​(ξ)​g),(A,B)=(g^{-1}\hat{A}_{u}Tg,g^{-1}\delta(\xi)g), (3.9)

where g∈U​(n)g\in\mathrm{U}(n) is an arbitrary unitary matrix whose last column uu satisfies the similarity relation

δ​(ξ)∼μ^​(y,u)​δ​(ξ),\delta(\xi)\sim\hat{\mu}(y,u)\delta(\xi), (3.10)

A^u∈SU​(n)\hat{A}_{u}\in\mathrm{SU}(n) is any fixed solution of (3.6), and T∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)T\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} is arbitrary.

Proof.

By Lemma 3.1, any (A,B)∈μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)(A,B)\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) has the form (3.9) since the solutions of (3.6) are the elements A^=A^u​T\hat{A}=\hat{A}_{u}T with a particular solution A^u\hat{A}_{u} and any T∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)T\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}.

To see the converse, consider (A,B)(A,B) given (3.9) and notice that

(A^u​T)​δ​(ξ)​(A^u​T)−1=μ^​(y,u)​δ​(ξ)(\hat{A}_{u}T)\delta(\xi)(\hat{A}_{u}T)^{-1}=\hat{\mu}(y,u)\delta(\xi) (3.11)

holds. Conjugating this by g−1g^{-1}, and defining (A,B)(A,B) by (3.9), gives

A​B​A−1=(g−1​μ^​(y,u)​g)​B,ABA^{-1}=(g^{-1}\hat{\mu}(y,u)g)B, (3.12)

which is just the condition (3.7) since μ^​(y,u)=g​μ0​(y)​g−1\hat{\mu}(y,u)=g\mu_{0}(y)g^{-1} for any g∈U​(n)g\in\mathrm{U}(n) having uu as its last column. Therefore, any (A,B)(A,B) given by (3.9) belongs to μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi). ∎

It is worth stressing that in Lemma 3.2 uu is only constrained by (3.10). For fixed uu, A^u\hat{A}_{u} is chosen, while g∈U​(n)g\in\mathrm{U}(n) still varies subject to gj​n=ujg_{jn}=u_{j}, ∀j\forall j. To control the fiber, we have to pin down the possible vectors uu and the possible matrices gg.

Lemma 3.3.

For fixed ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, let us choose a vector u0∈ℂnu_{0}\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n} that verifies the relation

δ​(ξ)∼μ^​(y,u0)​δ​(ξ).\delta(\xi)\sim\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\delta(\xi). (3.13)

Then, u∈ℂnu\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n} satisfies (3.10) if and only if it can be written in the form

u=X​u0withX∈U​(n)δ​(ξ).u=Xu_{0}\quad\hbox{with}\quad X\in\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. (3.14)
Proof.

The relations (3.13) and (3.14) imply immediately that u=X​u0u=Xu_{0} satisfies (3.10). We have to prove the converse, i.e., that any uu subject to (3.10) has the form in (3.14). For this purpose, note that the equality of the characteristic polynomials of δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) and μ^​(y,u)​δ​(ξ)\hat{\mu}(y,u)\delta(\xi) is equivalent to the similarity relation (3.10), and it can be spelled out as

∏j=1n(δj​(ξ)−ζ)=∏j=1n(δj​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ)+(e2​i​(1−n)​y−e2​i​y)​∑k=1n(|uk|2​δk​(ξ)​∏j=1j≠kn(δj​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ)).\prod_{j=1}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)-\zeta)=\prod_{j=1}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta)+(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}(1-n)y}-e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y})\sum_{k=1}^{n}\Bigl(|u_{k}|^{2}\delta_{k}(\xi)\prod_{\begin{subarray}{c}j=1\\ j\neq k\end{subarray}}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta)\Bigr). (3.15)

This results by calculating det(μ^​(y,u)−ζ​𝟏n)\det(\hat{\mu}(y,u)-\zeta{\mbox{$1$}}_{n}) using the well known identity det(𝟏n+v​wT)=1+wT​v\det({\mbox{$1$}}_{n}+vw^{T})=1+w^{T}v, which holds for any column vectors v,w∈ℂnv,w\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n}. Alternatively, (3.15) equivalent to the following equality of rational functions on the complex plane:

∏j=1nδj​(ξ)−ζδj​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ=1+(e2​i​(1−n)​y−e2​i​y)​∑k=1n|uk|2​δk​(ξ)δk​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ.\prod_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\delta_{j}(\xi)-\zeta}{\delta_{j}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta}=1+(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}(1-n)y}-e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y})\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{|u_{k}|^{2}\delta_{k}(\xi)}{\delta_{k}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta}. (3.16)

In turn, this can be rewritten as

∏j=1n(δj​(ξ)−ζ)−∏j=1n(δj​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ)∏j=1n(δj​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ)=(e2​i​(1−n)​y−e2​i​y)​∑k=1n|uk|2​δk​(ξ)δk​(ξ)​e2​i​y−ζ.\frac{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)-\zeta)-\prod_{j=1}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(\delta_{j}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta)}=(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}(1-n)y}-e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y})\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{|u_{k}|^{2}\delta_{k}(\xi)}{\delta_{k}(\xi)e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}-\zeta}. (3.17)

Consequently, ξ\xi must be such that the left-hand side has only first order poles, at the distinct values of δj​(x)2​i​y\delta_{j}(x)^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}, and uu is constrained by the equality of the residues at those poles.

If ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}, then nn distinct poles occur, and by comparing the residues one obtains

|uℓ|2=zℓ​(ξ,y),∀ℓ=1,…,n,|u_{\ell}|^{2}=z_{\ell}(\xi,y),\qquad\forall\ell=1,\dots,n, (3.18)

with the functions

zℓ​(ξ,y):=sin⁡(y)sin⁡(n​y)​∏j=1j≠ℓnei​y​δℓ​(ξ)−e−i​y​δj​(ξ)δℓ​(ξ)−δj​(ξ)=sin⁡(y)sin⁡(n​y)​∏j=ℓ+1ℓ+n−1[sin⁡(∑m=ℓj−1ξm−y)sin⁡(∑m=ℓj−1ξm)].z_{\ell}(\xi,y):=\frac{\sin(y)}{\sin(ny)}\prod^{n}_{\begin{subarray}{c}j=1\\ j\neq\ell\end{subarray}}\frac{e^{{\mathrm{i}}y}\delta_{\ell}(\xi)-e^{-{\mathrm{i}}y}\delta_{j}(\xi)}{\delta_{\ell}(\xi)-\delta_{j}(\xi)}=\frac{\sin(y)}{\sin(ny)}\prod_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+n-1}\left[\frac{\sin(\sum_{m=\ell}^{j-1}\xi_{m}-y)}{\sin(\sum_{m=\ell}^{j-1}\xi_{m})}\right]. (3.19)

Observe that these functions are strictly positive in the interior of the polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, where all the inequalities (2.25) hold in the strict sense. (In fact [21], the property zℓ​(ξ,y)>0z_{\ell}(\xi,y)>0, ∀ℓ\forall\ell characterizes the interior of the polytope 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}.)

Now, suppose that ξ∈𝒜y∩∂𝒜\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap\partial{\mathcal{A}}, i.e., δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) is not a regular element. Then, δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) has 1≤r<n1\leq r<n distinct eigenvalues, which we denote as Δ1​(ξ),…,Δr​(ξ)\Delta_{1}(\xi),\dots,\Delta_{r}(\xi). Furthermore, suppose that Δk​(ξ)\Delta_{k}(\xi) occurs with multiplicity mkm_{k}, m1+⋯+mr=nm_{1}+\cdots+m_{r}=n, and let ℳk⊂{1,…,n}{\mathcal{M}}_{k}\subset\{1,\dots,n\} be the subset of mkm_{k} indices satisfying

δj​(ξ)=Δk​(ξ),∀j∈ℳk.\delta_{j}(\xi)=\Delta_{k}(\xi),\quad\forall j\in{\mathcal{M}}_{k}. (3.20)

In this case, the equality of the residues gives

(e2​i​(1−n)​y−e2​i​y)​Δk​(ξ)​∑j∈ℳk|uj|2=−Resζ=ζk​F​(ζ,ξ,y),forζk:=e2​i​y​Δk​(ξ),(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}(1-n)y}-e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y})\Delta_{k}(\xi)\sum_{j\in{\mathcal{M}}_{k}}|u_{j}|^{2}=-\mathrm{Res}_{\zeta=\zeta_{k}}F(\zeta,\xi,y),\quad\hbox{for}\quad\zeta_{k}:=e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y}\Delta_{k}(\xi), (3.21)

where F​(ζ,ξ,y)F(\zeta,\xi,y) is the function that appears on the left-hand side of equation (3.17).

Notice that U​(n)δ​(ξ)=𝕋n\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}={\mathbb{T}}^{n}, the diagonal subgroup, if δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) is regular. If δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) is not regular, then (possibly up to an internal automorphism of U​(n)\mathrm{U}(n) that brings all equal eigenvalues of δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi) into neighbouring positions333This is needed if ξ1+⋯+ξj=π\xi_{1}+\cdots+\xi_{j}=\pi for some j≤(n−1)j\leq(n-1), since in such cases δ1​(ξ)=δn​(ξ)\delta_{1}(\xi)=\delta_{n}(\xi).) one has

U​(n)δ​(ξ)≃U​(m1)×U​(m2)×⋯×U​(mr).\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}\simeq\mathrm{U}(m_{1})\times\mathrm{U}(m_{2})\times\cdots\times\mathrm{U}(m_{r}). (3.22)

In either case, the form of the constraints (3.18) and (3.21) implies immediately that any two vectors u∈ℂnu\in{\mathbb{C}}^{n} that solve the similarity relation (3.10) can be transformed into each other by multiplication by an element of U​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. Indeed, this follows since any two ℂm{\mathbb{C}}^{m} vectors of the same length can be transformed into each other by an element of U​(m)\mathrm{U}(m). ∎

It is worth noting that the equality ∑ℓ=1n|uℓ|2=1\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}|u_{\ell}|^{2}=1 follows by evaluation of (3.15) at ζ=0\zeta=0 for any ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, and the sums ∑j∈ℳk|uj|2\sum_{j\in{\mathcal{M}}_{k}}|u_{j}|^{2} can be computed from (3.17), (3.21) for any ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. We shall also use that, in consequence of (3.18), ∑ℓ=1nzℓ​(ξ,y)=1\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}z_{\ell}(\xi,y)=1 holds for ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}.

Remark 3.4.

The formula (3.16) is a particular case of a so-called Weinstein-Aronszajn determinant, which also works for certain operators in Banach spaces, see [31]. If T=T0+VT=T_{0}+V is a finite rank perturbation of T0T_{0} then the resolvent R​(ζ,T)R(\zeta,T) of TT can be written in terms of the resolvent R​(ζ,T0)R(\zeta,T_{0}) of the unperturbed operator T0T_{0} as

R​(ζ,T)=R​(ζ,T0)−R​(ζ,T0)​V​(I+V​R​(ζ,T0))−1​R​(ζ,T0)R(\zeta,T)=R(\zeta,T_{0})-R(\zeta,T_{0})V(I+VR(\zeta,T_{0}))^{-1}R(\zeta,T_{0}) (3.23)

and ω​(ζ)=det(I+V​R​(ζ,T0))\omega(\zeta)=\det(I+VR(\zeta,T_{0})) is the Weinstein-Aronszajn determinant. It is a meromorphic function of ζ\zeta and its residues determine the spectral projections. The Laurent expansion of ω​(ζ)\omega(\zeta) has poles of higher order when the unperturbed operator has eigenvalues with multiplicity. The simplest case of a rank one perturbation leads to the present formula (3.16). The Weinstein-Aronszajn determinant has been used to great effect in the work of Moser [36] on integrable systems.

Lemma 3.5.

Keeping u0u_{0} from Lemma 3.3, let g0g_{0} be a unitary matrix having u0u_{0} as its last column. Then, the unitary matrices with last column u=X​u0u=Xu_{0} (3.14) are given by the expression

g=X​g0​η−1,g=Xg_{0}\eta^{-1}, (3.24)

where η∈U​(n)\eta\in\mathrm{U}(n) verifies ηj,n=ηn,j=δj,n\eta_{j,n}=\eta_{n,j}=\delta_{j,n} for all j=1,…,nj=1,\dots,n.

Proof.

Any unitary matrix gg can be written as in (3.24) with η=g†​X​g0\eta=g^{\dagger}Xg_{0}. If gg and X​g0Xg_{0} have the same last column, then we have

ηj,n=∑k=1ng¯k,j​(X​g0)k,n=∑k=1ng¯k,j​gk,n=δj,n,\eta_{j,n}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bar{g}_{k,j}(Xg_{0})_{k,n}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bar{g}_{k,j}g_{k,n}=\delta_{j,n}, (3.25)

because gg is unitary. Then, the relation ηn,j=δn,j\eta_{n,j}=\delta_{n,j} follows from the unitarity of η\eta. It is also obvious that gg and X​g0Xg_{0} have the same last column if ηj,n=δj,n\eta_{j,n}=\delta_{j,n} is valid. ∎

Remark 3.6.

Note that η\eta in (3.24) satisfies η​μ0​(y)​η−1=μ0​(y)\eta\mu_{0}(y)\eta^{-1}=\mu_{0}(y) and conjugation by η\eta is the same as conjugation by η~∈SU​(n)μ0​(y)\tilde{\eta}\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} given by η~=c​η\tilde{\eta}=c\eta with any c∈ℂc\in{\mathbb{C}} verifying cn=(detη)−1c^{n}=(\det\eta)^{-1}.

Definition 3.7.

For any fixed ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, choose a unitary matrix g0g_{0} whose last column u0u_{0} satisfies the similarity relation δ​(ξ)∼μ^​(y,u0)​δ​(ξ)\delta(\xi)\sim\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\delta(\xi) and also choose an element A^0≡A^u0∈SU​(n)\hat{A}_{0}\equiv\hat{A}_{u_{0}}\in\mathrm{SU}(n) verifying A^0​δ​(ξ)​A^0−1=μ^​(y,u0)​δ​(ξ)\hat{A}_{0}\delta(\xi)\hat{A}_{0}^{-1}=\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\delta(\xi). Then, define the subset M​(ξ)⊂β−1​(ξ)∩μ−1​(μ0​(y))M(\xi)\subset\beta^{-1}(\xi)\cap\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) by

M​(ξ):={(g0−1​A^0​T​g0,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)∣T∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)},M(\xi):=\{(g_{0}^{-1}\hat{A}_{0}Tg_{0},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0})\mid T\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}\}, (3.26)

and introduce the closed Lie subgroups

SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0:={η∈SU​(n)∣η​δ​(ξ)​η−1=δ​(ξ),η​μ^​(y,u0)​η−1=μ^​(y,u0)}<SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)^{u_{0}}_{\delta(\xi)}:=\{\eta\in\mathrm{SU}(n)\mid\eta\delta(\xi)\eta^{-1}=\delta(\xi),\,\eta\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\eta^{-1}=\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\}<\mathrm{SU}(n) (3.27)

and

Kξg0:=g0−1​SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0​g0<SU​(n)μ0​(y).K^{g_{0}}_{\xi}:=g_{0}^{-1}\mathrm{SU}(n)^{u_{0}}_{\delta(\xi)}g_{0}<\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}. (3.28)
Remark 3.8.

We suppressed the dependence of M​(ξ)M(\xi) on the choices of g0g_{0} and A^0\hat{A}_{0}, which will turn out to be irrelevant, as is further discussed in Appendix C. The notations introduced in (3.27) and (3.28) are just convenient shorthands introduced in order to economize on writing out the intersections of isotropy groups:

SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0≡SU​(n)δ​(ξ)∩SU​(n)μ^​(y,u0)andKξg0≡SU​(n)g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0∩SU​(n)μ0​(y).\mathrm{SU}(n)^{u_{0}}_{\delta(\xi)}\equiv\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}\cap\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})}\quad\hbox{and}\quad K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\equiv\mathrm{SU}(n)_{g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}}\cap\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}. (3.29)

Later we shall use that SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\mathrm{SU}(n)^{u_{0}}_{\delta(\xi)} consists of the elements of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} that have u0u_{0} as an eigenvector. This holds since u0​u0†=η​u0​(η​u0)†u_{0}u_{0}^{\dagger}=\eta u_{0}(\eta u_{0})^{\dagger} for η∈U​(n)\eta\in\mathrm{U}(n) is equivalent to η​u0=Γ​u0\eta u_{0}=\Gamma u_{0} for some Γ∈U​(1)\Gamma\in\mathrm{U}(1).

Lemma 3.9.

The action of the subgroup Kξg0<SU​(n)μ0​(y)K^{g_{0}}_{\xi}<\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} (3.28) preserves M​(ξ)M(\xi) (3.26). Even more, if (A,B)∈M​(ξ)(A,B)\in M(\xi) and η∈SU​(n)μ0​(y)\eta\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} are such that (η​A​η−1,η​B​η−1)(\eta A\eta^{-1},\eta B\eta^{-1}) belongs to M​(ξ)M(\xi), then η∈Kξg0\eta\in K^{g_{0}}_{\xi}.

Proof.

According to Lemma 3.2, M​(ξ)M(\xi) contains all elements (A,B)∈μ−1​(μ0​(ξ))∩β−1​(ξ)(A,B)\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(\xi))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) for which B=g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0B=g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}, which immediately implies the first statement. Notice that if (A,B)(A,B) and (η​A​η−1,η​B​η−1)(\eta A\eta^{-1},\eta B\eta^{-1}) are in M​(ξ)M(\xi), then η∈g0−1​SU​(n)δ​(ξ)​g0\eta\in g_{0}^{-1}\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}g_{0}. Since Kξg0=g0−1​SU​(n)δ​(ξ)​g0∩SU​(n)μ0​(y)K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}=g_{0}^{-1}\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}g_{0}\cap\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}, the second statement follows. ∎

Lemma 3.10.

Let us make M​(ξ)M(\xi) (3.26) into a smooth manifold via its parametrization by the elements of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. Then, the mapping

jξ:M​(ξ)→μ−1​(μ0​(y))j_{\xi}:M(\xi)\to\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) (3.30)

given by the tautological injection is a smooth embedding. The restriction of the action of SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} on M​(ξ)M(\xi) yields a smooth action

Kξg0×M​(ξ)→M​(ξ),K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\times M(\xi)\to M(\xi), (3.31)

and the quotient space M​(ξ)/Kξg0M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} admits a unique smooth manifold structure for which the canonical projection πξ:M​(ξ)→M​(ξ)/Kξg0\pi_{\xi}:M(\xi)\to M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} is a smooth submersion.

Proof.

Consider the chain of maps

M​(ξ)​⟶jξ​μ−1​(μ0​(y))​⟶ι0​P,M(\xi)\overset{j_{\xi}}{\longrightarrow}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\overset{\iota_{0}}{\longrightarrow}P, (3.32)

and notice that the composed map ι0∘jξ\iota_{0}\circ j_{\xi} is an embedding. This implies that the map jξj_{\xi} is smooth. One may show this directly, or by applying the universal property of initial submanifolds to μ−1​(μ0​(y))⊂P\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\subset P, since every embedded submanifold is also an initial submanifold [40].

The smooth map D​jξDj_{\xi} has full rank at every point of M​(ξ)M(\xi), since the map D​(ι0∘jξ)D(\iota_{0}\circ j_{\xi}) has full rank at every point of M​(ξ)M(\xi), because ι0∘jξ\iota_{0}\circ j_{\xi} is an embedding. The map jξj_{\xi} is obviously injective, and thus it is an injective immersion. It is then an embedding, since every injective immersion of a compact manifold is an embedding [33].

The action map Kξg0×M​(ξ)→μ−1​(μ0​(y))K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\times M(\xi)\to\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) is smooth, since it is the restriction of the action map SU​(n)μ0​(y)×μ−1​(μ0​(y))→μ−1​(μ0​(y))\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\times\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\to\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) on an embedded submanifold.

The claim about the smooth manifold structure of the quotient space M​(ξ)/Kξg0M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} follows from standard results [33, 40], since the isotropy group of every point of M​(ξ)M(\xi) is the center ℤn<SU​(n){\mathbb{Z}}_{n}<\mathrm{SU}(n). ∎

We note in passing that for ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}} the manifold M​(ξ)M(\xi) is an orbit of the free 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action as given in (2.17).

Lemma 3.11.

The parametrization of M​(ξ)M(\xi) (3.26) by SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} leads to an identification

M​(ξ)/Kξg0≡SU​(n)δ​(ξ)/SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0.M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\equiv\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}. (3.33)

The quotient space on the right-hand side refers to the group action

SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0×SU​(n)δ​(ξ)→SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}\times\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}\to\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} (3.34)

given by the map

(X,T)↦Θ0​(X)​T​X−1,∀(X,T)∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0×SU​(n)δ​(ξ),(X,T)\mapsto\Theta_{0}(X)TX^{-1},\qquad\forall(X,T)\in\mathrm{SU}(n)^{u_{0}}_{\delta(\xi)}\times\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}, (3.35)

where

Θ0​(X):=A^0−1​X​A^0∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ).\Theta_{0}(X):=\hat{A}_{0}^{-1}X\hat{A}_{0}\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. (3.36)

The manifold (3.33) is compact and connected.

Proof.

This is a straightforward consequence of the equality

(g0−1​X​g0)​(g0−1​A^0​T​g0)​(g0−1​X​g0)−1=g0−1​(A^0​Θ0​(X)​T​X−1)​g0,(g_{0}^{-1}Xg_{0})(g_{0}^{-1}\hat{A}_{0}Tg_{0})(g_{0}^{-1}Xg_{0})^{-1}=g_{0}^{-1}(\hat{A}_{0}\Theta_{0}(X)TX^{-1})g_{0}, (3.37)

which ensures that the group actions (3.31) and (3.34) are intertwined by the equivariant diffeomorphism

Kξg0×M​(ξ)∋(g0−1​X​g0,(g0−1​A^0​T​g0,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0))↦(X,T)∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0×SU​(n)δ​(ξ).K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\times M(\xi)\ni(g_{0}^{-1}Xg_{0},(g_{0}^{-1}\hat{A}_{0}Tg_{0},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}))\mapsto(X,T)\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}\times\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. (3.38)

The fact that Θ0​(X)\Theta_{0}(X) belongs to SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} is readily checked from the definitions. The quotient space (3.33) is compact and connected since SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} has these properties. Indeed, it is well known ([9], Theorem 1 in Section 9.5.3) that every isotropy subgroup for the adjoint action of a connected and simply connected compact Lie group on itself is connected. ∎

Remark 3.12.

The group SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} contains the center ℤn<SU​(n){\mathbb{Z}}_{n}<\mathrm{SU}(n), and ℤn{\mathbb{Z}}_{n} gives the isotropy group of every point of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} with respect to the action of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} given by the formula (3.35). Indeed, this is a consequence of the fact that the action of SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} on μ−1​(μ0​(y))\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) is free except for the trivial action of the center ℤn{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}

Before turning to the characterization of the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi), we need one more piece of preparation. Let 𝔰​𝔲​(n)μ0​(y){\mathfrak{su}}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} and 𝔨ξg0{\mathfrak{k}}_{\xi}^{g_{0}} denote the Lie algebras of the groups SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} and Kξg0K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}, respectively. For any Z∈𝔰​𝔲​(n)μ0​(y)Z\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} and p=(A,B)∈μ−1​(μ0​(y))p=(A,B)\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) denote

Zp:=([Z,A],[Z,B])∈Tp​μ−1​(μ0​(y)).Z_{p}:=([Z,A],[Z,B])\in T_{p}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)). (3.39)

This expression describes the vector fields induced by the action of SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}.

Lemma 3.13.

If ZpZ_{p} belongs to Tp​M​(ξ)⊂Tp​μ−1​(μ0​(y))T_{p}M(\xi)\subset T_{p}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) for some Z∈𝔰​𝔲​(n)μ0​(y)Z\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} and p∈M​(ξ)p\in M(\xi), then Z∈𝔨ξg0Z\in{\mathfrak{k}}_{\xi}^{g_{0}}.

Proof.

If ZpZ_{p} belongs to Tp​M​(ξ)T_{p}M(\xi), then we must have [Z,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0]=0[Z,g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}]=0, which means that Z=g0−1​Υ​g0Z=g_{0}^{-1}\Upsilon g_{0} for some element Υ∈𝔰​𝔲​(n)δ​(ξ)\Upsilon\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. But Υ\Upsilon must also satisfy [Υ,μ^​(y,u0)]=0[\Upsilon,\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})]=0, since [Z,μ0​(y)]=0[Z,\mu_{0}(y)]=0. This means that Z∈𝔨ξg0Z\in{\mathfrak{k}}_{\xi}^{g_{0}}. ∎

Theorem 3.14.

Let us define the map

F:M​(ξ)/Kξg0→μ−1​(μ0​(y))/SU​(n)μ0​(y)F:M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\to\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} (3.40)

by the prescription

F:Kξg0⋅(A,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)↦SU​(n)μ0​(y)⋅(A,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0),∀(A,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)∈M​(ξ).F:K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\cdot(A,g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0})\mapsto\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\cdot(A,g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}),\quad\forall(A,g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0})\in M(\xi). (3.41)

This map is a smooth injective immersion whose image is β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi).

Proof.

We begin by showing that every orbit of SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} in μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) admits representatives in M​(ξ)M(\xi), which means that the image of the map FF is β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi). We know that all elements (A,B)∈β−1​(ξ)∩μ−1​(μ0​(y))(A,B)\in\beta^{-1}(\xi)\cap\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) are given by the formula (3.9). In this formula, according to Lemmas 3.3 and 3.5, we can write u=X​u0u=Xu_{0} with X∈U​(n)δ​(ξ)X\in\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} and substitute (3.24) for gg, which gives

(A,B)=(η​g0−1​X−1​A^X​u0​T​X​g0​η−1,η​g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0​η−1).(A,B)=(\eta g_{0}^{-1}X^{-1}\hat{A}_{Xu_{0}}TXg_{0}\eta^{-1},\eta g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}\eta^{-1}). (3.42)

One can check directly from the definitions that for any X∈U​(n)δ​(ξ)X\in\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} the element

X′:=A^0−1​X−1​A^X​u0X^{\prime}:=\hat{A}_{0}^{-1}X^{-1}\hat{A}_{Xu_{0}} (3.43)

belongs to U​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{U}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. By using this, we can rewrite (3.42) as

(A,B)=(η​g0−1​A^0​T′​g0​η−1,η​g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0​η−1)withT′=X′​T​X∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ).(A,B)=(\eta g_{0}^{-1}\hat{A}_{0}T^{\prime}g_{0}\eta^{-1},\eta g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}\eta^{-1})\quad\hbox{with}\quad T^{\prime}=X^{\prime}TX\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}. (3.44)

As observed in Remark 3.6, here η\eta can be replaced by η~∈SU​(n)μ0​(y)\tilde{\eta}\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}. Since (η−1​A​η,η−1​B​η)∈M​(ξ)(\eta^{-1}A\eta,\eta^{-1}B\eta)\in M(\xi), every SU​(n)μ0​(y)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} orbit in μ−1​(μ0​(y))∩β−1​(ξ)\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\cap\beta^{-1}(\xi) has representatives in M​(ξ)M(\xi).

To see that FF (3.41) is injective, suppose that

SU​(n)μ0​(y)⋅(A1,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)=SU​(n)μ0​(y)⋅(A2,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\cdot(A_{1},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0})=\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\cdot(A_{2},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}) (3.45)

for two elements of M​(ξ)M(\xi). This means that there exists some η∈SU​(n)μ0​(y)\eta\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} that transforms (A1,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)(A_{1},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}) into (A2,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)(A_{2},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}). We have shown in Lemma 3.9 that any such η\eta necessarily belongs to Kξg0K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}, whereby the Kξg0K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} orbits of (A1,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)(A_{1},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}) and (A2,g0−1​δ​(ξ)​g0)(A_{2},g_{0}^{-1}\delta(\xi)g_{0}) are the same.

To verify the smoothness of FF (3.41), notice that M​(ξ)M(\xi) is a locally trivial (left) principal fiber bundle over M​(ξ)/Kξg0M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}, with structure group Kξg0/ℤnK_{\xi}^{g_{0}}/{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}. Thus, given any point x0∈M​(ξ)/Kξg0x_{0}\in M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}, there exists a smooth local section σ:Ux0→M​(ξ)\sigma:U_{x_{0}}\to M(\xi) over a coordinate neighbourhood Ux0U_{x_{0}} of x0x_{0}. It is clear that jξ∘σj_{\xi}\circ\sigma (see (3.30)) is also smooth, and the restricted map F|Ux0F_{|U_{x_{0}}} can be written as

F|Ux0=π0∘jξ∘σ,F_{|U_{x_{0}}}=\pi_{0}\circ j_{\xi}\circ\sigma, (3.46)

where π0:μ−1​(μ0​(y))→μ−1​(μ0​(y))/SU​(n)μ0​(y)\pi_{0}:\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))\to\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y))/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)} is the canonical projection. Smoothness is a local property, and F|Ux0F_{|U_{x_{0}}} is obviously smooth.

It remains to show that FF (3.41) is an immersion, i.e., its derivative D​F​(x0)DF(x_{0}) is injective at every point of x0∈M​(ξ)/Kξg0x_{0}\in M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}. Take an arbitrary non-zero tangent vector X∈Tx0​(M​(ξ)/Kξg0)X\in T_{x_{0}}(M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}). We have to show that (D​F​(x0))​(X)≠0(DF(x_{0}))(X)\neq 0. Choosing a local section σ:Ux0→M​(ξ)\sigma:U_{x_{0}}\to M(\xi) as in the previous step of the proof, let us put p:=σ​(x0)p:=\sigma(x_{0}). Since D​jξ​(p)Dj_{\xi}(p) is the trivial inclusion of Tp​M​(ξ)T_{p}M(\xi) into Tp​μ−1​(μ0​(y))T_{p}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)), by using (3.46) we can write

(D​F​(x0))​(X)=(D​π0​(p))​((D​σ​(x0))​(X)).(DF(x_{0}))(X)=(D\pi_{0}(p))((D\sigma(x_{0}))(X)). (3.47)

The kernel of D​π0​(p)D\pi_{0}(p) is the tangent space of the orbit SU​(n)μ0​(y)⋅p\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}\cdot p at pp. Therefore, if (D​F​(x0))​(X)=0(DF(x_{0}))(X)=0, then

(D​σ​(x0))​(X)=Zpfor someZ∈𝔰​𝔲​(n)μ0​(y).(D\sigma(x_{0}))(X)=Z_{p}\quad\hbox{for some}\quad Z\in{\mathfrak{su}}(n)_{\mu_{0}(y)}. (3.48)

Here, we used the notation (3.39). But (D​σ​(x0))​(X)(D\sigma(x_{0}))(X) belongs to Tp​M​(ξ)<Tp​μ−1​(μ0​(y))T_{p}M(\xi)<T_{p}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)). Consequently, by Lemma 3.13, Z∈𝔨ξg0Z\in{\mathfrak{k}}_{\xi}^{g_{0}} must hold, which entails that ZpZ_{p} is tangent Kξg0⋅pK_{\xi}^{g_{0}}\cdot p. However, because σ\sigma is a section, (D​σ​(x0))​(X)(D\sigma(x_{0}))(X) is not tangent to the Kξg0K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} orbit through pp for any non-zero XX. (Indeed, the section induces a local trivialization πξ−1​(Ux0)≃Ux0×Kξg0/ℤn\pi_{\xi}^{-1}(U_{x_{0}})\simeq U_{x_{0}}\times K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}/{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}, and in this trivialization σ\sigma becomes the map Ux0∋x↦(x,e)U_{x_{0}}\ni x\mapsto(x,e), where ee is the unit element of the structure group. Then, D​σ​(x0)​(X)D\sigma(x_{0})(X) can be written as (X,0)(X,0), while the Kξg0K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} orbit reads (x0,Kξg0/ℤn(x_{0},K_{\xi}^{g_{0}}/{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}.) This proves that (D​F​(x0))​(X)≠0(DF(x_{0}))(X)\neq 0 for any non-zero tangent vector, i.e., FF is an immersion. ∎

Theorem 3.15.

The map FF (3.40) is an embedding of the manifold M​(ξ)/Kξg0M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} into P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)). Its image is the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi), which is therefore a connected embedded submanifold of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)).

Proof.

We have shown that FF is an injective immersion and its image is β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi). Hence, it is an embedding as M​(ξ)/Kξg0M(\xi)/K_{\xi}^{g_{0}} is compact. The connectedness follows from Lemma 3.11. ∎

We finish by showing that β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is an isotropic submanifold of the symplectic manifold (P​(μ0​(y)),ωμ0​(y))(P(\mu_{0}(y)),\omega_{\mu_{0}(y)}). This will follow from the following lemma.

Lemma 3.16.

The pull-back of the quasi-Hamiltonian 2-form ω\omega (2.4) vanishes on M​(ξ)M(\xi). That is, with the tautological embedding ιξ:M​(ξ)→P\iota_{\xi}:M(\xi)\to P, we have

ιξ∗​(ω)=0.\iota_{\xi}^{*}(\omega)=0. (3.49)
Proof.

In practice, ιξ∗​(ω)\iota_{\xi}^{*}(\omega) is obtained by substituting (A,B)∈M​(ξ)(A,B)\in M(\xi) into the formula (2.4). Since in this case BB is constant, the first two terms vanish. By using that A​B=μ0​(y)​B​AAB=\mu_{0}(y)BA, we get

ιξ∗​(ω)=−⟨(μ0​(y)​B​A)−1​d​(μ0​(y)​B​A),∧(B​A)−1​d​(B​A)⟩=−⟨A−1​d​A,∧A−1​d​A⟩,\iota_{\xi}^{*}(\omega)=-\langle(\mu_{0}(y)BA)^{-1}\mathrm{d}(\mu_{0}(y)BA)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\wedge}}{{,}}(BA)^{-1}\mathrm{d}(BA)\rangle=-\langle A^{-1}\mathrm{d}A\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\wedge}}{{,}}A^{-1}\mathrm{d}A\rangle, (3.50)

which vanishes obviously. ∎

Theorem 3.17.

The fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is an isotropic submanifold.

Proof.

First, we need to recall the key property of the symplectic form ωμ0​(y)\omega_{\mu_{0}(y)}. To do so, let us fix an arbitrary point z∈P​(μ0​(y))z\in P(\mu_{0}(y)) and two arbitrary tangent vectors V,W∈Tz​P​(μ0​(y))V,W\in T_{z}P(\mu_{0}(y)). Then, pick a point z~∈μ−1​(μ0​(y))\tilde{z}\in\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) and two vectors V~,W~∈Tz~​μ−1​(μ0​(y))\tilde{V},\tilde{W}\in T_{\tilde{z}}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) for which z=π0​(z~)z=\pi_{0}(\tilde{z}) and

D​π0​(z~)​(V~)=VandD​π0​(z~)​(W~)=W.D\pi_{0}(\tilde{z})(\tilde{V})=V\quad\hbox{and}\quad D\pi_{0}(\tilde{z})(\tilde{W})=W. (3.51)

The equality (2.1) means that

ωμ0​(y)​(V,W)=ω​(V~,W~).\omega_{\mu_{0}(y)}(V,W)=\omega(\tilde{V},\tilde{W}). (3.52)

Now, suppose that zz, V,WV,W are such that z∈β^−1​(ξ)z\in\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) and V,WV,W are tangent to β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi). In this case, we can choose z~∈M​(ξ)\tilde{z}\in M(\xi) and V~,W~∈Tz~​M​(ξ)⊂Tz~​μ−1​(μ0​(y))\tilde{V},\tilde{W}\in T_{\tilde{z}}M(\xi)\subset T_{\tilde{z}}\mu^{-1}(\mu_{0}(y)) so that (3.51) holds. This is easily seen from the proof of the claim that FF (3.40) is an immersion with its image being β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi). But then the ω​(V~,W~)=0\omega(\tilde{V},\tilde{W})=0 by Lemma 3.16, and hence the equality (3.52) implies the claim. ∎

Theorems 3.14, 3.15 and 3.17 represent the main results of the present paper. Their message is that the Liouville integrable systems of [21] exhibit ‘spherical singularities’ in all type (ii) cases, enriching the set of known integrable systems for which similar singularities were found previously [7, 8, 12, 13, 32, 38, 39]. The rather concrete description of the fibers, as given by Lemma 3.11, is also an important result.

3.2. Recovering the torus structure of β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) for ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}

Although the structure of the fibers for ξ∉(∂𝒜y)sing\xi\notin(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}} is known [21], it appears worth explaining how it arises as an easy consequence of Theorem 3.15, which is valid for any ξ∈𝒜y\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. To do so, we shall apply the identification β^−1​(ξ)=SU​(n)δ​(ξ)/SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi)=\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} provided by Lemma 3.11 and Theorem 3.15.

Corollary 3.18.

Let us suppose that ξ∈𝒜y∩𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}} and from the nn functions zℓz_{\ell} (ℓ=1,…,n\ell=1,\dots,n) in (3.19) zℓ​(y,ξ)=0z_{\ell}(y,\xi)=0 holds for a unique dd-element subset, for some 0≤d≤(n−1)0\leq d\leq(n-1). Then, the fiber is a torus

β^−1​(ξ)≃𝕋n−1−d.\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi)\simeq{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1-d}. (3.53)
Proof.

After choosing ξ\xi, fix also a corresponding vector u0u_{0}. It follows from (3.18) that the number of zero components of u0u_{0} is dd. Since ξ∈𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}, we have

SU​(n)δ​(ξ)=𝕋n−1.\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}={\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}. (3.54)

As noted before, if in addition ξ∈𝒜yo\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o}, then zℓ​(y,ξ)>0z_{\ell}(y,\xi)>0 for all ℓ\ell, and thus d=0d=0. In this case, the action (3.35) of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0=ℤn\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}={\mathbb{Z}}_{n} is trivial, and thus

β^−1​(ξ)≡SU​(n)δ​(ξ)/SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0=𝕋n−1,∀ξ∈𝒜yo,\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi)\equiv\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}/\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}={\mathbb{T}}^{n-1},\qquad\forall\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o}, (3.55)

which is a standard Liouville torus.

If ξ∈(∂𝒜y)reg\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{reg}}, then the vector u0u_{0} has 1≤d≤(n−1)1\leq d\leq(n-1) zero components. This holds because zℓ​(y,ξ)z_{\ell}(y,\xi) vanishes precisely if one of the sums in (2.25) is equal to yy, for the respective index ℓ\ell, and such equalities characterize the boundary ∂𝒜y\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y}. In this case, SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} is a dd-dimensional torus. For example, if the first dd components of u0u_{0} are zero, then X∈SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0X\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} has the form

X=diag​(τ1,…,τd,τ,…,τ)withτd=(τ1​⋯​τd−1​τn−d)−1,X=\mathrm{diag}(\tau_{1},\dots,\tau_{d},\tau,\dots,\tau)\quad\hbox{with}\quad\tau_{d}=(\tau_{1}\cdots\tau_{d-1}\tau^{n-d})^{-1}, (3.56)

and thus X∈SU​(n)u0X\in\mathrm{SU}(n)_{u_{0}} is parametrized by (τ1,…,τd−1,τ)∈𝕋d(\tau_{1},\dots,\tau_{d-1},\tau)\in{\mathbb{T}}^{d}. Now, since SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} is Abelian, we can define the homomorphism

φ:SU(n)δ​(ξ)u0→SU(n)δ​(ξ)=𝕋n−1,φ(X):=Θ0(X)X−1=A^0−1XA^0X−1.\varphi:\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}\to\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}={\mathbb{T}}^{n-1},\quad\varphi(X):=\Theta_{0}(X)X^{-1}=\hat{A}_{0}^{-1}X\hat{A}_{0}X^{-1}. (3.57)

Then, the action (see (3.35)) of SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}} on SU​(n)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)} works according to the rule

(X,T)↦T​φ​(X),(X,T)\mapsto T\varphi(X), (3.58)

and we know that this action is free, except for the trivial action of the center ℤn<SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}<\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}. By using this, the formula (3.58) of the action implies that the kernel of the homomorphism φ\varphi is ℤn{\mathbb{Z}}_{n} (which is not apparent from the formula (3.57) of φ\varphi). It follows that the image

φ​(SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0)<𝕋n−1\varphi(\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}})<{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} (3.59)

is a dd-dimensional sub-torus (since all connected, compact, Abelian Lie groups are tori). As a result, the fiber gets identified with the coset space

β^−1​(ξ)=𝕋n−1/φ​(SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0)≃𝕋n−1−d.\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi)={\mathbb{T}}^{n-1}/\varphi(\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}})\simeq{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1-d}. (3.60)

Here, we used that the quotient space (in fact, factor group) of a torus by a sub-torus is again a torus. ∎

Recall that the momentum map β^\hat{\beta} generates a Hamiltonian 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action on

P​(μ0​(y))reg:=β^−1​(𝒜y∩𝒜reg),P(\mu_{0}(y))^{\mathrm{reg}}:=\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}), (3.61)

which is a dense open submanifold of P​(μ0​(y))P(\mu_{0}(y)). This action descends from the free action (2.17) on β−1​(𝒜reg)\beta^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}) and it remains free on β^−1​(𝒜yo)\hat{\beta}^{-1}({\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o}), since SU​(n)δ​(ξ)u0=ℤn\mathrm{SU}(n)_{\delta(\xi)}^{u_{0}}={\mathbb{Z}}_{n} for ξ∈𝒜yo\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}_{y}^{o} (3.1).

The restricted system on P​(μ0​(y))regP(\mu_{0}(y))^{\mathrm{reg}} is quite similar to compact toric systems [5, 14]. For example, let us suppose that ξ\xi is a ‘regular vertex’ of 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y}, i.e., a vertex that belongs to 𝒜y∩𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}_{y}\cap{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}. It follows from standard results on polytopes [41, 48] that ξ\xi must satisfy at least (n−1)(n-1) of the 2​n2n defining relations (2.25) as equalities. Notice ξℓ+⋯+ξℓ+k−1=y\xi_{\ell}+\cdots+\xi_{\ell+k-1}=y and ξℓ+⋯+ξk=y\xi_{\ell}+\cdots+\xi_{k}=y cannot hold together since ξ∈𝒜reg\xi\in{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}}. By (3.19), either of these equalities imply that zℓ​(ξ,y)=0z_{\ell}(\xi,y)=0. Taking also into account that ∑ℓ=1nzℓ​(ξ,y)=1\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}z_{\ell}(\xi,y)=1, we conclude that any regular vertex satisfies precisely (n−1)(n-1) of the defining relations as equalities. Since our polytope is of dimension (n−1)(n-1), it also follows that the pertinent (n−1)(n-1) equalities correspond to faces of co-dimension one. This means that the regular vertices behave in the same way as the vertices of the so-called simple polytopes [41], which contain the Delzant polytopes [5, 14] of toric systems as a subclass.

We see from the above discussion and Corollary 3.24 that β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) is a fixed point of the torus action for any regular vertex ξ\xi. We remark that there is also a correspondence between different isotropy types of the Hamiltonian 𝕋n−1{\mathbb{T}}^{n-1} action and the intersections between 𝒜reg{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathrm{reg}} and faces of 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} of different dimensions, like in toric systems. In particular, one can show that if ξ∈(∂𝒜y)reg\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{reg}} belongs to the relative interior of a face of co-dimension dd, then the points of β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) possess dd-dimensional isotropy groups and are tori of dimension (n−1−d)(n-1-d) (3.53).

The characteristic property of the type (ii) cases is that β^\hat{\beta} does not generate a global torus action, since (∂𝒜y)sing≠∅(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}\neq\emptyset. The simplest examples of ‘singular fibers’, i.e., fibers associated with ξ∈(∂𝒜y)sing\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}, are presented in the next section.

4. The type (ii)\mathrm{(ii)} examples of lowest dimension

We wish to apply the combination of Lemma 3.11 and Theorem 3.15 for describing the fiber β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) for ξ∈(∂𝒜y)sing\xi\in(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}} (3.2). Such values of ξ\xi first occur for n=4n=4, for any

π3<y<π2.\frac{\pi}{3}<y<\frac{\pi}{2}. (4.1)

The corresponding momentum polytope was already calculated444The polytope was given in [21] without describing the calculation. The result is confirmed by Theorem 5.1 and Remark 5.2 below, which include it as the n=4n=4 special case. in [21]. We next present it in detail, and then describe the singular fibers.

To avoid confusion, let us stress that the true momentum map is the map (2.21), but the nn-th component is often included for notational convenience. In our case, we write the points of the polytope as 4-component vectors, but Figure 2 refers to their projection onto the 3-dimensional space of the first 3 components.

4.1. The n=4n=4 momentum polytope

Our 3-dimensional momentum polytopes have 8 vertices, 4 lying in (∂𝒜y)reg(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{reg}} and another 4 in (∂𝒜y)sing(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}. To display them, we employ the cyclic permutation ν:=σ−1\nu:=\sigma^{-1} (2.27). The four regular vertices are

R​(1)=(y,π−2​y,3​y−π,π−2​y)andR​(k)=νk−1​(R​(1))fork=2,3,4,R(1)=(y,\pi-2y,3y-\pi,\pi-2y)\quad\hbox{and}\quad R(k)=\nu^{k-1}(R(1))\quad\hbox{for}\quad k=2,3,4, (4.2)

and the four ‘singular’ (irregular) vertices are

I​(1)=(y,π−2​y,y,0)andI​(k)=νk−1​(I​(1))fork=2,3,4.I(1)=(y,\pi-2y,y,0)\quad\hbox{and}\quad I(k)=\nu^{k-1}(I(1))\quad\hbox{for}\quad k=2,3,4. (4.3)

It follows that

(∂𝒜y)sing={I​(1),I​(2),I​(3),I​(4)}.(\partial{\mathcal{A}}_{y})^{\mathrm{sing}}=\{I(1),I(2),I(3),I(4)\}. (4.4)

The polytope also has 8 faces, four are quadrilateral and another four are triangular The quadrilateral face 𝒬​(k){\mathcal{Q}}(k) is defined by the equation ξk+ξk+1=y\xi_{k}+\xi_{k+1}=y (modulo 4). With the above labelling of the vertices we have

𝒬​(1)=⟨I​(2),R​(3),I​(3),R​(4)⟩,{\mathcal{Q}}(1)=\langle I(2),R(3),I(3),R(4)\rangle, (4.5)

meaning that 𝒬​(1){\mathcal{Q}}(1) is the convex hull of the 4 listed vertices, which are listed in the order of positive orientation as seen from the outside. From this, one also sees the edges that are incident with 𝒬​(1){\mathcal{Q}}(1). The other quadrilateral faces are 𝒬​(k)=νk−1​(𝒬​(1)){\mathcal{Q}}(k)=\nu^{k-1}({\mathcal{Q}}(1)) for k=2,3,4k=2,3,4.

The triangular face 𝒯​(k){\mathcal{T}}(k) is defined by the equality ξk=y\xi_{k}=y. For example, we have

𝒯​(1)=⟨I​(1),R​(1),I​(3)⟩and𝒯​(k)=νk−1​(𝒯​(1))fork=2,3,4,{\mathcal{T}}(1)=\langle I(1),R(1),I(3)\rangle\quad\hbox{and}\quad{\mathcal{T}}(k)=\nu^{k-1}({\mathcal{T}}(1))\quad\hbox{for}\quad k=2,3,4, (4.6)

with the vertices listed anti-clockwise. It should be noted that the application of the cyclic permutation changes the orientation. For example, the vertices of 𝒯​(2)=⟨I​(2),R​(2),I​(4)⟩{\mathcal{T}}(2)=\langle I(2),R(2),I(4)\rangle are listed clockwise, and similarly for 𝒬​(2){\mathcal{Q}}(2).

Every irregular vertex is incident with 4 faces, 2 triangular and 2 quadrilateral, and with 4 edges. For example, we have

I​(1)=𝒯​(1)∩𝒯​(3)∩𝒬​(3)∩𝒬​(4)I(1)={\mathcal{T}}(1)\cap{\mathcal{T}}(3)\cap{\mathcal{Q}}(3)\cap{\mathcal{Q}}(4) (4.7)

and

I​(1)=⟨I​(1),R​(1)⟩∩⟨I​(1),R​(2)⟩∩⟨I​(1),R​(3)⟩∩⟨I​(1),I​(3)⟩.I(1)=\langle I(1),R(1)\rangle\cap\langle I(1),R(2)\rangle\cap\langle I(1),R(3)\rangle\cap\langle I(1),I(3)\rangle. (4.8)

Every regular vertex is incident with 3 faces and with 3 edges. For example, we have

R​(1)=𝒯​(1)∩𝒬​(2)∩𝒬​(3)R(1)={\mathcal{T}}(1)\cap{\mathcal{Q}}(2)\cap{\mathcal{Q}}(3) (4.9)

and

R​(1)=⟨R​(1),I​(1)⟩∩⟨R​(1),I​(3)⟩∩⟨R​(1),I​(4)⟩.R(1)=\langle R(1),I(1)\rangle\cap\langle R(1),I(3)\rangle\cap\langle R(1),I(4)\rangle. (4.10)

The other incidence relations follow by the application of the cyclic permutation ν\nu.

Remark 4.1.

One can check that the 3 edges emanating from any regular vertex satisfy the conditions of a Delzant polytope. Namely, they are parallel to 3 vectors that have integer components and form a basis of the lattice ℤ3{\mathbb{Z}}^{3}. Equivalently, the normal vectors of the 3 faces incident with a regular vertex can be normalized so that they form a basis of ℤ3{\mathbb{Z}}^{3}.

Remark 4.2.

The above remark refers to the projection to the space of the first 3 components, but actually the full symmetry of the geometric object 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} is better seen from the perspective of the Euclidean hyperlane E⊂ℝ4E\subset{\mathbb{R}}^{4} (2.24). There, the following ‘reconstruction’ is available. First, replace the 4 vertices R​(k)R(k) by new vertices R​(k)′R(k)^{\prime} given by

R​(1)′:=diag​(π2,π2−y,2​y−π2,π2−y),R(1)^{\prime}:=\mathrm{diag}(\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}-y,2y-\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}-y), (4.11)

and its cyclic permutations R​(k)′=νk−1​(R​(1))R(k)^{\prime}=\nu^{k-1}(R(1)) for k=2,3,4k=2,3,4. With respect to the distance in EE, this yields a rectangular parallelepiped, whose faces

⟨I​(1),R​(1)′,I​(3),R​(3)′⟩and⟨R​(2)′,I​(4),R​(4)′,I​(2)⟩\langle I(1),R(1)^{\prime},I(3),R(3)^{\prime}\rangle\quad\hbox{and}\quad\langle R(2)^{\prime},I(4),R(4)^{\prime},I(2)\rangle (4.12)

are squares of edge length (π−2​y)(\pi-2y) and the other two edges of Q​(1)′=⟨I​(2),R​(3)′,I​(3),R​(4)′⟩Q(1)^{\prime}=\langle I(2),R(3)^{\prime},I(3),R(4)^{\prime}\rangle and its permutations have length (4​y−π)(4y-\pi). Then, 𝒜y{\mathcal{A}}_{y} is reconstructed by chopping off the new vertices along opposite diagonals of the squares, in such a way that results in equilateral triangles 𝒯​(k){\mathcal{T}}(k) in EE.

4.2. The n=4n=4 singular fibers

We below describe the structure of β^−1​(ξ)\hat{\beta}^{-1}(\xi) for ξ=I​(2)\xi=I(2) given by

ξ=(0,y,π−2​y,y)for whichδ​(ξ)=−i​diag​(1,1,e2​i​y,e−2​i​y).\xi=(0,y,\pi-2y,y)\quad\hbox{for which}\quad\delta(\xi)=-{\mathrm{i}}\,\mathrm{diag}(1,1,e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y},e^{-2{\mathrm{i}}y}). (4.13)

The other 3 singular fibers have the same structure.

Now we have

SU​(4)δ​(ξ)={T=diag​(Γ,Γ3,Γ4)∣Γ∈U​(2),Γ3,Γ4∈U​(1),Γ3​Γ4​det(Γ)=1}≃U​(2)×U​(1).\mathrm{SU}(4)_{\delta(\xi)}=\{T=\mathrm{diag}(\Gamma,\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4})\mid\Gamma\in\mathrm{U}(2),\,\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4}\in\mathrm{U}(1),\,\Gamma_{3}\Gamma_{4}\det(\Gamma)=1\}\simeq\mathrm{U}(2)\times\mathrm{U}(1). (4.14)

By inspecting the equality (3.17), we find that the corresponding unit vectors u∈ℂ4u\in{\mathbb{C}}^{4} are constrained by

|u1|2+|u2|2=−1/(2​cos⁡(2​y)),|u3|2=1+1/(2​cos⁡(2​y)),u4=0.\quad|u_{1}|^{2}+|u_{2}|^{2}=-1/(2\cos(2y)),\,\,|u_{3}|^{2}=1+1/(2\cos(2y)),\,\,u_{4}=0. (4.15)

For the application of Lemma 3.11, we choose the vector u0u_{0} as

u0=(0,r,1−r2,0)Twithr:=−1/(2​cos⁡(2​y)).u_{0}=(0,r,\sqrt{1-r^{2}},0)^{T}\quad\hbox{with}\quad r:=\sqrt{-1/(2\cos(2y))}. (4.16)

Note that rr and 1−r2\sqrt{1-r^{2}} are positive. Then, the group SU​(u)ξu0<SU​(4)δ​(ξ)\mathrm{SU}(u)_{\xi}^{u_{0}}<\mathrm{SU}(4)_{\delta(\xi)} can be displayed as

SU​(4)ξu0={X=diag​(a,b,b,c)∣a,b,c∈U​(1),a​b2​c=1}≃U​(1)×U​(1).\mathrm{SU}(4)_{\xi}^{u_{0}}=\{X=\mathrm{diag}(a,b,b,c)\mid a,b,c\in\mathrm{U}(1),\,ab^{2}c=1\}\simeq\mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{U}(1). (4.17)

Next, we have to fix A^0∈SU​(4)\hat{A}_{0}\in\mathrm{SU}(4) that satisfies A^0​δ​(ξ)=μ^​(y,u0)​A^0\hat{A}_{0}\delta(\xi)=\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\hat{A}_{0}. With u0u_{0} (4.16), we can write

μ^​(y,u0)​δ​(ξ)=−i​diag​(e2​i​y,e2​i​y,e4​i​y,1)−diag​(0,Q,0),\hat{\mu}(y,u_{0})\delta(\xi)=-{\mathrm{i}}\,\mathrm{diag}(e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y},e^{2{\mathrm{i}}y},e^{4{\mathrm{i}}y},1)-\mathrm{diag}(0,Q,0), (4.18)

with a 2 by 2 matrix QQ that we do not need to spell out. A brief inspection of the equation
